policy_statement: Governor-directed policy: DEC must adopt regulations requiring all new passenger cars, pickup trucks, and SUVs sold in New York State to be zero-emission by 2035, modeled on California's Advanced Clean Cars II.
mechanism_description: The policy directs DEC to adopt regulations that will require new passenger vehicles sold to be zero-emission, increasing ZEV adoption and reducing transportation greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Vehicle sales regulation → ↑ ZEV market share → ↓ fossil fuel combustion in road transport → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Automakers/dealers and consumers are required/induced to sell and purchase ZEVs for new passenger vehicles, transforming the vehicle fleet over time.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Electrification (or hydrogen use) displaces direct combustion of gasoline/diesel in vehicles; emissions shift to (typically lower-carbon) electricity generation/hydrogen production and decline further as grids decarbonize.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle CO₂e emissions from the light-duty transport sector (especially tailpipe CO₂), lowering overall GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing transportation GHG emissions by replacing internal-combustion vehicle miles with zero-emission vehicle miles.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Regulation increases ZEV share of new vehicle sales → fleet electrifies → less fossil fuel burned in transport → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can also improve local air quality and reduce petroleum use (resource/security co-benefits), the principal climate-relevant impact is direct emissions mitigation from reduced combustion.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity/hydrogen supply were very carbon-intensive, benefits could be reduced, but the mechanism’s intended and typical dominant effect remains transport GHG mitigation; adaptation is not the primary aim.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any ZEV sales standard primarily functions by shifting vehicle technology away from combustion to reduce transport emissions over the fleet turnover period.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Details like lifecycle emissions accounting, grid decarbonization linkage, equity design, and charging buildout are policy-specific and should be handled at the policy level, not mechanism-level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions reductions depend on vehicle uptake compliance, fleet turnover speed, and electricity/hydrogen carbon intensity; complementary infrastructure and affordability measures affect realized outcomes but do not change the dominant mitigation classification.
key_indicators: “directed DEC to adopt regulations”; “require all new passenger cars, pickup trucks, and SUVs sold… be zero-emission by 2035”; “modeled on California’s Advanced Clean Cars II”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (NOx/PM) and associated public health benefits; reduced petroleum dependence/energy security benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Although ACC II is often implemented via credit-based performance compliance across manufacturers, the statement is framed as an outright requirement; classified as a regulatory mandate rather than a performance standard due to the explicit “require… be zero-emission” phrasing.
